data.title: Sell Nike: Overpriced On Pandemic-Era Performance And Turnaround Hopes
data.description:
This article attempts to compare NIKE (NKE) against its two much smaller but formidable peers in the running shoe sector, namely Deckers Outdoor (DECK) and On Holdings AG (ONON), review its performance over the last 10 years, and draw from there a somewhat informed inference as to whether Nike stock represents a buy or not.

Overview of the Three Companies

Let's first have a quick overview of the sizes of the three companies in terms of market cap, annual revenue and workforce. (All tables herein are compiled from the Peers function of Seeking Alpha, except the 10-year selective data table is compiled from Seeking Alpha's income statements for NIKE).

NKE

DECK

ONON

Market Cap

76.05B

13.35B

10.72B

TTM Revenue

46.5B

5.4B

3.8B

TTM Net Income

2.5B

1.0B

257M

Employees

77,800

5,500

3,963

Revenue Per Employee

$598K

$982K

$959K

Net Income Per Employee

$32K

$182K

$64K

NIKE commands ~6-7 times the market cap of DECK and ONON, and ~8-12 times the TTM revenue of them. However, although it commands ~10 times the TTM net income of ONON, its TTM net income is only ~2.5 times that of DECK.

Yet NIKE has a workforce that is ~15-20 times that of its two smaller rivals. Consequently, its revenue and net income per employee lag behind them by a wide margin, which probably suggests it is a mature and bloated organization.

Valuation of the Three Companies

As measured by stock price to various earning measures, whether TTM or forward earnings, GAAP or non-GAAP earnings, NIKE is valued higher by ~50% to ~150% than DECK and by ~50% than ONON. This pattern repeats itself in terms of free cash flow.

NKE

NIKE is undervalued by ~50% in terms of price/sales, and that is probably due to its lower gross and net profitability (see below).

Growth Trajectories of the Three Companies

Other than growing its revenue at the similar rates of inflation during the last 5 years or so, NIKE has no meaningful growth in terms of revenue, EBITDA or net income to speak of, whether during the last 3 years, or YOY, or in the next 12 months.

However, DECK and ONON are growing their revenues, EBITDA and net incomes across the board virtually all by double digits, whether during the last 3 years or 5 years, or YOY, or in the next 12 months.

Profitability Profiles of the Three Companies

Now you may wonder why Mr. Market has assigned much higher price to earning ratios to NIKE whose business is not really growing. As you can tell from the table below, it is not because NIKE is more profitable than DECK or ONON, either.

As a matter of fact, NIKE lags DECK and ONON by ~16% - 20% in gross margin, ~6% - 17% in EBITDA margin. While it manages to outperform ONON on return on equity by ~4.5%, it lags behind DECK by a wide margin of over 20% there. In terms of return on total assets and total capital, the gaps there are similarly wide.

Nike’s 10 Year Financial Performance

Now that we have finished comparing Nike against its much smaller but apparently more profitable and faster-growing peers, whose stocks are also valued much lower, let's review NIKE's own performance during the last 10 years to see how NIKE has arrived at this point, and whether NIKE is likely to turn around to "deserve" its apparently higher valuation.

You can see from the table above that before the Pandemic, NIKE is not really a growth stock any more. Its revenues and gross profits were growing at single digit and EPS hovered between $1.17 and $2.51.

Between 2020 and 2022, Nike’s revenue rose from $37.4 billion to $46.7 billion, and then to $51.2 billion by 2023. Net income peaked at over $6 billion and EPS peaked at $3.75.

This period coincided with an extraordinary surge in demand for athletic wear, fueled by lockdowns, stimulus checks, and a shift toward casual clothing as people work from home. Nike’s stock, effectively on “steroids” during this time, peaked at around $170, trading at roughly 45× earnings—an unmistakable sign that Mr. market was pricing the temporary conditions as if they were permanent.

At the same time, Nike accelerated its direct-to-consumer strategy. By cutting out wholesalers and pushing sales through its own digital channels, it expanded margins and reinforced the perception that it was becoming a more efficient, higher-quality and growth business.

But those gains apparently proved to be more transient than sustainable. Revenue has since declined from $51.3 billion in 2024 to approximately $46.5 billion today. Net income has fallen from $5.7 billion to $2.5 billion. Gross profit has declined by over 12% year-over-year in the most recent period. In other words, a substantial portion of the pandemic-era gains has reversed.

What was priced as structural improvement of its business turned out primarily a function of temporary demand and favorable conditions.

What Now?

The recent underperformance of both NIKE's business and stock has not resulted from a cyclical wobble, as DECK and ONON have both been growing at double digits during the same time period. If you look at the above table, especially the YOY growth rates, you can tell that this is a business that has stopped growing and has seen meaningful earnings erosion. Furthermore, it is clearly not cutting its SG&A enough in the face of revenue decline.

However, NIKE's stock valuation has not yet fully adjusted to its slower business growth (if there is any ongoing growth at all), or its subpar profitability profile despite the fact that it had declined from its peak level around $170s to low $50s now, essentially where the stock traded in 2015. Why? it still trades at ~30× earnings and over 20× EBITDA.

That is the first and most important disconnect between the stock price and lackluster business fundamentals, unless Mr. Market is essentially betting on a quick and meaningful turnaround of its business.

What does that turnaround look like? and how likely can NIKE pull it out? What must happen to support Nike’s current valuation and then a modest amount of appreciation of its stock?

At the current 30× earnings, even assuming a normalized multiple of 20×, Nike would need to grow earnings materially just to prevent multiple compression from eroding shareholder returns.

If earnings were to remain flat at $2.5 billion, a re-rating from 30× to 20× alone would imply a one-third decline in equity value. It is psychologically difficult to accept that given the stock price has declined so much already from its $170s level to today's $50s. However, over the long term, fourth-grade math will trump human psychology.

On the other side of the coin, to sustain its current valuation (let's put aside any material stock price appreciation first), Nike would need to grow back earnings toward its prior peak of ~$5–6 billion, to >$4 billion (note its current market cap is ~$75 billion). That would require not only a recovery in revenue, but probably also a significant expansion in margins back toward double digits.

That is a tall order for a company of this scale that is operating in an increasingly competitive market. The apparently bloated workforce at NIKE creates a challenge to turn around such a large ship. The current macro-economic conditions, particularly consumer sentiments in the U.S. and elsewhere probably will not help NIKE in this endeavor, either.

I am not saying it is impossible, but it is a tall order for the company. At least based upon the 10-year data table above, I have not seen meaningful signs of a turnaround yet.

My overall prediction, if I have to venture one here, is that NIKE's stock will continue to hover around ~$40-$75 for the foreseeable future, depending on its own business performance and market sentiments. This is consistent with its 10-year EPS ranges between $1.17 and $3.75 above, limited growth potential and low profitability.

Conclusion

Capital should be allocated to businesses that are growing faster and/or more profitable than its peers, and are priced fairly relative to its probable potential to grow and earn profits to compensate for risks.

Nike is a large, mature company with declining earnings, subpar margins relative to peers, yet its stock is traded at a higher valuation that apparently assumes a degree of recovery that we can only hope for.

As stated in this previous article of mine covering primarily DECK,

I no longer wish to bet on such a turnaround [of NIKE] (or a turnaround of large-cap companies in general).

The reason is simple: whether lifting such a large ship can be done is far more difficult than an average individual investor can intelligently assess. Equally importantly, NIKE's sheer size of $90 billion market cap works against it, particularly for individual investors seeking nimble small- or mid-cap opportunities capable of delivering five- or ten-fold returns in the next 5-10 years. How can NIKE sell that many more sneakers to the world?

For the above reasons, I rate NIKE a SELL even though NIKE's market cap has declined further down to ~$75B now.

data.title: CAVA: Expansion Plans Justify Valuation
data.description:
In late 2025, I stated that Cava Group, Inc. (CAVA) was a “Buy.” I thought this founder-led company was outperforming similar fast casual peers like Chipotle (CMG) and Sweetgreens (SG) while creating new initiatives that I thought would position the business well in the long run.

Since that article, CAVA is up over 25% while the S&P 500 is down nearly 7%. To many, these types of quick corrections may lead investors to again believe this stock is overvalued.

I remain bullish on this business and I’m a believer in the long-term prospects of this company. Let’s dive into the most recent quarterly results as I share my perspective on this growth story.

Aggressive Expansion Plans

CAVA remains on track with their aggressive expansion plans as the company plans to open between 74-76 new locations in fiscal year 2026 with the ultimate goal of getting to 1,000 restaurants by 2032.

In 2026, CAVA will look to add stores in many Midwest cities including Cincinnati, Minneapolis, St. Louis, and Columbus. Over seven years (assuming the goal is hit at the end of 2032), CAVA would need to grow their store count by 12.5% annually. In 2025, CAVA opened 72, bringing their total store count to 439, which is nearly a 20% increase. I'd say that's impressive considering many competitors such as Sweetgreen have struggled to increase traffic and deliver a profitable bottom line (which I noted in another article), meanwhile CAVA has managed increase store count while growing revenue and maintaining positive same-store sales.

While 1,000 may sound like a big number when you compare that to the 3,938 restaurants Chipotle has in the United States you can see CAVA has a large amount of white space.

To support these expansion plans, management noted a few key initiatives, one of which I mentioned in my prior article was “Flavor Your Feature.” This is the company’s initiative to build out the company’s new generation of leaders. The company recently hired Doug Thompson to be the company’s Chief Operations Officer and he'll be responsible for helping to execute this initiative. One of the first steps within this initiative is adding more assistant general managers (AGMs). While it’s early days, CEO Bret Schulman noted the positives from AGMs on the company’s latest earnings call stating:

While still early, we're seeing encouraging signs that this approach is having a positive impact. Restaurants with AGM coverage are outperforming those without as AGMs provide additional leadership support during peak dinner and weekend shifts, helping to strengthen execution across every shift, develop future team members and create more sustainable restaurant teams.

The company’s management team also noted numerous technology enhancements including new kitchen display screen systems (KDS) and TurboChef ovens which will help ensure quality and consistency in offerings. Also, these will be a key aspect of future offerings such as the company’s pomegranate-glazed salmon which will be launching soon.

Lastly, in an effort to connect with guests and keep them coming back associated with the company’s new tiered loyalty program is “Oasis” which is the company’s invite-only tier. Oasis will give CAVA’s most loyal customers more perks, earning opportunities as well as exclusive merchandise.

While these all seem to be excellent plans to embolden the company’s continued expansion plans, risks certainly remain which I’ll discuss next.

Risks

CAVA lists numerous items that might impact the business going forward. I’m going to touch on a few risks noted in the company’s Risk Factors section in their most recent10K filing.

As I noted in an article about a similar growth restaurant company in Dutch Bros, aggressive expansion plans certainly increase operational risks. CAVA could have difficulties finding suitable real estate and securing financing. Then there’s the risk of hiring appropriately but hopefully that risk will be mitigated with the Flavor Your Feature initiative.

Competition remains a top risk , especially in this type of macro environment where consumers are being far more selective as the costs of dining out outpace the costs of eating food at home according to data from McKinsey. Further McKinsey data goes on to say consumers are making less frequent visits and are more sensitive to price increases. One positive for CAVA is that compared to other fast casual chains, the company has been less reliant on menu increases to drive revenue growth.

Lastly, I’d argue brand reputation damage is a risk for the business, especially if there’s an issue with the food. One notable example is the E. Coli issue Chipotle experienced years ago. Chipotle managed to turn things around but in today’s very competitive environment having such issues would certainly hurt the company’s reputation and could dampen the business’s expansion plans.

Financials

Before diving into the quarterly reports let’s zoom out and see how well CAVA has performed on an annual basis over the last few years.

As you can see below operating expenses have increased each year but revenue growth has outpaced these costs resulting in an increase to income from operations as well as an increase in income before taxes:

In fiscal year 2025, revenue grew by 22.5%. Same store sales increased by 4% and the company opened 72 new stores bringing the total number of restaurants to 439. You can see net income decreased in 2025 but that was due to a net benefit from the release of the valuation allowance against deferred tax assets. Excluding that amount, the comparable adjusted net income for fiscal year 2024 would be $50.2, which would result in a 26.9% increase in 2025.

CAVA shareholders can appreciate that dilution was very minimal in 2025 compared to 2024.

In Q4 2025, CAVA generated revenue of roughly $273 million which is a 21.2% increase compared to Q4 2024. Same sale growth for the quarter was nearly flat at 0.5%. CAVA’s restaurant level profit was $58.3 million for the quarter, which is a 15.7% increase compared to the prior year fourth quarter. Restaurant-level profit margin was 21.4%. The company opened 24 new restaurants in the quarter.

CAVA provided the following guidance for fiscal year 2026:

CAVA still has a solid balance sheet as you can see below with enough current assets to cover all of their current liabilities and a shrinking accumulated deficit balance:

Valuation

CAVA has a valuation grade of a “F” at Seeking Alpha:

When looking at these metrics, whichever way you choose to value CAVA it’s hard not to argue it’s not expensive. As CAVA is still in its growth phase, I continue to believe the best valuation metric for this business is EV/Sales or Price-to-Sales. While CAVA's forward P/S is much higher than the sector median, it's still significantly lower than compared to this time last year:

Now let's look at CAVA's valuation from different angle having some assumptions about their expansion plans. Let’s assume CAVA hits their 1,000 store goal by 2032 and they’re able to continue to hold 4% same-store sales growth, which I think is conservative considering 2025 was a rough year for restaurants. Let’s also assume CAVA is able to scale and have some EBITDA margin expansion and let’s assume new stores open at higher AUVs (let’s say $3M plus). Using those inputs you’d get an implied share price of $180 as you can see in my model below:

In this model, CAVA would end up trading at 35X EBITDA. If CAVA kept trading at a premium like 45X or 55X EBITDA in 2032, then like a Chipotle, that’d certainly be a more compelling case. Still, with some base (if not conservative assumptions) I still think the case for CAVA is compelling.

No doubt CAVA's valuation is still expensive compared to established industry peers, I believe a premium is warranted in CAVA’s case as analysts expect CAVA to continue to grow revenue by over 20% annually over the next several years.

Conclusion

CAVA has rebounded since my prior article and it’s certainly understandable to say the company appears overvalued. Yet, if you’re like a long-term investor like I am then I think it’s easier to justify today’s prices given the company’s expansion plans.

Compared to industry peers, CAVA has proven they’re able to increase same stores sales even if a difficult macro setting and I believe they are making appropriate plans like their technology enhancements and AGM initiative to set themselves up in a winning position for years to come.

I’d urge investors to take the long view and assess the stock for it’s potential because if management can execute on their 1,000 store rollout by 2032, I’d say investors along for the ride will be quite pleased with their returns.

data.title: Micron: AI Memory Paradox Could Send Demand Exploding Despite Margin Risks
data.description:
Micron Technology (MU) has bucked the trend gaining 4.9% since my last report while the S&P 500 lost 8.3%. The company is seeing strong growth in sales driven by robust pricing in DRAM as well as NAND driven by robust AI demand. The stock, however, retreated 24% from its highs amid broader concerns on AI investments. From fundamental perspective, I believe that there was 13% upside to $382.41 bases on earnings expectations for FY27 and 25% to $420.34 bases on earnings expectations for FY28. So, the stock ran substantially ahead of itself in my view. In this report, I discuss the company’s Q2 2026 earnings and update my price target.

Sales And Margins Surge On Broad-Based Pricing Strength

The second quarter results surpassed analyst revenues by a staggering $4.1 billion putting on display AI driven volume and pricing strength for memory in NAND and DRAM technology with a substantial increase in gross, profit and net income margins.

Second quarter revenues rose 75% sequentially and 196% year-over-year to $23.9 billion. Nearly 80% of the revenues or $18.8 billion came from DRAM including high bandwidth memory with $5 billion coming from NAND revenues as SSD demand has substantially increased. The sequential volume uplift was not spectacular with bit shipments up mid-single digits for DRAM and low-single digits for NAND. It shows that sequentially pricing power substantially increased. DRAM pricing was up mid-60% while NAND was up high-70%. That tells us the story of supply chain constraints boosting pricing power for Micron.

Cloud Memory revenues grew 46.7% sequentially and 163% year-on-year to $7.75 billion with gross margins rising 8 points sequentially and 19 points year-on-year to 74% with an 11 points sequential and 21 points year-on-year lift in operating margins to 66%. Core Data Center as the pure AI/data center segment saw 139% growth sequentially and 210% growth year-on-year to $5.7 billion with gross margins expanding from 47% last year and 51% in the prior quarter to 74% and similarly operating margins increased from 33% last year and 37% in the prior quarter to 67%. Interesting to note is that HBM margins remain below non-HBM margins. Seeing such a strong margin expansion despite the margin dilution from HBM is definitely a strong sign. Mobile and Client revenues rose 245% year-over-year and 81.2% sequentially to $7.7 billion with gross margins expanding from 15% and 54% to 79% and from 1% and 47% to 76%. Automotive and Embedded revenues grew 57% sequentially and 162% year-on-year to $2.7 billion which is strong growth given that the automotive industry is facing some output challenges. Gross margins rose from 21% last year and 45% in the prior quarter to 68% while operating margins rose from 6% and 36% to 62%.

Micron Technology Guide Remains Impressive

What we are currently seeing is an extremely strong pricing environment as supply is heavily constrained. That does not only lift pricing in the core data center segment and for HBM memory modules, but across the board and across all product classes and technologies. That is a good sign, but it also puts a marker that may have investors somewhat concerned and that is if supply improves, the pricing among all product classes and technologies will normalize. Right now, investors are reassessing AI investments and while I do believe that AI is here to stay and it will continue growing, the macro outlook may increase data center construction costs, increase raw material costs and reduce AI budgets for smaller companies. I generally believed that AI is a faster grower for a strong economic growth setting and in a lower economic growth setting it also has its use cases but perhaps less efficient as a cost cutter than a growth multiplier.

Furthermore, we note that with high pricing HBM potentially may have compressed timelines to be accretive to margins and Google has introduced an algorithm called TurboQuant that substantially reduces memory use. That obviously has investors concerned, but it is part of the innovation trajectory I would say. As AI scales, memory becomes the bottleneck and engineers develop quantization and pruning methods to reduce memory consumption. As a result, efficiency and cost-efficiency improve, memory chips become cheaper and AI can growth efficiently again and memory demand increases again. So, innovations in memory chips and algorithms are the driver of AI growth.

This is also known as the Jevons paradox where technological advancement increases resource efficiency to reduce costs/resource demand leading to higher overall consumption rather than lower consumption. So, while TurboQuant may look like a bad thing for investors of Micron Technology, it is not.

Micron is also committing an additional $10 billion in FY27 to increase output and also that may be seen as headwind for pricing power, but follows the principle that lower memory prices can increase overall demand and then again provide pricing power. That should lead to a better combination of volume growth and pricing power.

For the third quarter, the company guides for $32.75 billion to $34.25 billion in sales with gross margins at 81% indicating six-point sequential step up in margins. What investors were likely looking for definite comment that the company is sold out through 2027, which would provide a runway to assume strong pricing power. However, there was no explicit mention of this.

The company, however, did provide the following comment during the Q2 earnings call:

Yes. I think we obviously don't provide any kind of price modeling or anything else type of discussion in that forward-looking way. But we have mentioned that the demand forecast that we get from our customers for 2026, for 2027 continue to escalate. And despite efforts that we are making to increase the amount of supply we can bring online to a modest extent in '26, but some more in '27 is not really making that much of a meaningful dent in the gap. There is a lot of demand growth driven by AI and also across different segments of the market, not just in the data center. And we expect tight conditions for the foreseeable future and certainly beyond 2026 and not giving any further guidance beyond that.

It is the strongest indication together with increased construction CapEx that we can expect multi-year strength driven by supply chain constraints against robust demand. However, the last line of the remark puts the relevant of the comment mostly on 2026 “and then some” and that may have given investors a wrong rub since generally it is already known that 2026 is more or less sold out and whatever additional volumes come available for shipment are largely through efficiency gains. So, the comment on forward pricing power was just not strong enough and I believe that investors were looking for higher conviction on pricing power from management.

Micron Technology Stock Remains A Strong Buy

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

The revisions on the estimates on EBITDA and free cash flow are simply mind blowing. I have no other way to describe it. EBITDA estimates more than doubled for 2026-2028 with an 84% lift to this year’s estimate, 138% for next year and 94% for 2028. Free cash flow estimates have increased 144% with an 81% lift for this year, 165% for next year and 174% for the year after. Even when we price the company at 6.7x EV/EBITDA, which is lower than the 3-year median of >14x and lower than the 23x for Semiconductor peers, the upside remains massive. I am revising my stock price target to $556.40 indicating 56% in the base case and $871.56 in the bullish case indicating 144% upside.

Driven by the supply chain bottlenecks, margins are expected to increase to north of 80% this year and increase to around 86% by 2027. Interesting to note is that for 2028, a reduction in EBITDA is expected and that would coincide with more capacity coming online so some of the pricing power may disappear. However, we also note that the AI demand outlooks constantly evolve so as we had further into the year we may also see more visibility on demand for 2028. Free cash flow will have another year of massive growth beyond 2026 and that has given the company the confidence to increase dividends by 30% and use its net cash position to tender $5.4 billion in notes.

Conclusion: Micron Technology’s Upside Is On A Whole Different Level

At a time when markets are trading down, Micron has been able to buck the trend though we do note that the stock price has been sliding in recent session on AI investment and memory demand concern on algorithm optimization. We also note that by FY28, the current estimates indicate that some pricing power may disappear. However, I do believe that technological innovation is also required on algorithms level to ascertain continued AI growth and while reduction of pricing power seems to be a net negative by FY28 I do believe that for a healthy growth outlook having a more balanced supply and demand system is required. It does mean that pricing power is expected to decline over time but that should bolster the cost efficiency of AI infrastructure paving the way for volume growth. So, I do not expect the >80% margins to hold, but the stock seems to be substantially underpriced at current levels measured against the expected earnings and cash flow as well as on EV/EBITDA level against the broader reference group and the company’s 3-year median EV/EBITDA.

data.title: Texas Capital Bancshares: No Common Dividend, But A Preferred Dividend
data.description:
Introduction

Texas Capital Bancshares (TCBI) is a Texas-based company and the holding company of the Texas Capital Bank, which ended 2025 with an excess of $31.5 billion in assets on its balance sheet. In a previous article, I was focused on the bank’s preferred shares as I thought they provided a nice option for the fixed-income portion of my portfolio. The risk/reward ratio was pretty good, and I now want to have another look at these securities.

A strong 2025 sets the scene for 2026

Before discussing the details of the preferred stock, it makes sense to take a step back and take a look at the bank’s financial results in 2025.

As you can see below, the total interest income increased by more than 2% to $1.77 billion, and although that sounds like a relatively low percentage increase, keep in mind this went hand in hand with a 10% decrease in the interest expenses. This ultimately resulted in a net interest income of approximately $1.03 billion, which is a 14% increase compared to the NII in the preceding year. Another important element is the substantial reduction in the loan loss provisions, which reached the lowest level in several years.

The bank also saw its non-interest income increase pretty sharply, but before getting too excited, the large difference is predominantly caused by the loss on the sale of securities recorded in 2024, which makes a comparison pretty difficult. That being said, I am very pleased to see the investment banking and advisory fees remain strong.

Meanwhile, the bank was also able to keep its operating expenses under control, and this resulted in a pre-tax income of almost $433 million. The net income reported in 2025 was $330 million, with $313 million of that net income attributable to the common shareholders of Texas Capital Bancshares. This resulted in an EPS of $6.86.

From the perspective of a preferred shareholder, it is great to see the bank needed just over 5% of its net income to cover the preferred dividends. This low payout ratio, of course, is a positive feature.

During the year, TCBI also bought back its own shares. As you can see below, the $200 million share buyback program was completed in the fourth quarter, repurchasing 2.25 million shares.

A new $200 million share buyback program is currently active, so I think it’s fair to assume the bank’s share count will continue to decrease.

And thanks to the strong financial performance in 2025 and the lack of a dividend, the book value increased, while the total amount of common equity, which acts as a first buffer for the preferred equity in case things go wrong, increased. Texas Capital Bancshares ended 2025 with $3.63 billion in equity, which means there’s approximately $3.3 billion in common equity, which ranks junior to the $300 million in preferred stock that’s currently outstanding.

The price of the preferred shares moved up, but they are still attractive

Texas Capital Bancshares has one series of preferred stock outstanding, which is trading with (TCBIO) as its ticker symbol. There are 12 million preferred shares outstanding for a total preferred capital of $300 million. With a preferred dividend coupon of 5.75%, this provides TCBI with a relatively low cost of capital. This works out to $1.4375 per preferred share, payable in four equal quarterly installments of just under $0.36 per share.

These securities are non-cumulative in nature (the bank does not have to make up for skipped preferred dividend payments), and are callable from June 15 on.

But with a 5.75% coupon, I think this still is a pretty cheap cost of capital for Texas Capital. And even if the bank would call the preferred shares, no one would really object to an immediate 10%+ capital gain based on the current share price.

Investment thesis

While my main focus was and still is on the preferred shares issued by Texas Capital Bancshares, I still think the common shares are attractive. At the end of 2025, the stock had a tangible book value of around $75 per share, which means the current multiple of less than 1.3 times TBV is still relatively attractive, despite the relatively high earnings multiple based on the 2025 results. While I currently have no position in the common shares of Texas Bancshares, I am keeping an eye on the common shares as well.

My main focus remains on the bank’s preferred stock. I am somewhat surprised to see the preferred share price move up as interest rates on the financial markets are also increasing, but with a preferred dividend yield of approximately 6.5% based on the current stock price, I still think the risk/reward ratio is very decent. And as the bank doesn’t pay a dividend on its common shares, TCBI can act faster if things go wrong, to protect its balance sheet.

data.title: Broadcom: A Great Company At A Fair Price
data.description:
Introduction

A lot has happened for Broadcom (AVGO) since I last rated it a Buy back in early September 2025 and I want to revisit my thesis as to see if the company can still lead the charge in this new AI world, as it posted a return of negative 4% since despite having two blowout quarters in a row.

Current Dynamics

I’ll first go through the latest earnings as to provide a quick snapshot of what has been happening at the firm. Broadcom posted a double beat in its fiscal Q1 with Non-GAAP EPS coming out to $2.05, a slight beat of 3 cents, while revenue came out to a staggering $19.31B, a 29.4% Y/Y growth rate and a beat of $170MM. This was largely linked to the robust demand for custom AI accelerators and AI networking, with the overall AI revenue growth accelerating. That’s why the company has guided for AI revenue to be $10.7B in Q2 and overall revenue is set to come to $22B, ahead of the consensus of $20.4B, while EBITDA margin is set to come out to 68%.

More so, management has set a clear line of sight to achieve AI revenue in excess of $100B by fiscal 2027, which would be a giant leap from the reported $20B in AI sales reported during the whole fiscal 2025. Though this is actually credible considering the 140% Y/Y target of $10.7B for the incoming quarter. This is largely linked to the CapEx environment, where hyperscalers are projected to spend around $630B on AI infrastructure in 2026 alone.

That is particularly highlighted by the number of partnerships it has signed for custom accelerators, in which Broadcom holds around 60% market share as of 2026. This dominance is a result of manufacturing capacity as well as great IP and design expertise that allows hyperscalers to develop highly efficient hardware for their specific software stacks. These XPUs are meant to eliminate the massive weight of Nvidia (NVDA) in the industry, in which its GPUs have unnecessary features relative to inference or training.

One of those partnerships has been the one with Anthropic (ANTHRO) which has emerged as a major revenue driver with the AI lab committing a total of $21B for custom chip orders that will result in the delivery of nearly 1MM TP units and bring over 1 gigawatt of new compute capacity online. What is important in this deal is that it bypasses cloud intermediaries, allowing Anthropic to secure massive AI scale via Broadcom’s direct supply channel while still utilizing the Google-designed TPU architecture. The same type of deal was signed with OpenAI through Project Titan which leaves AVGO exposed to the two biggest independent giants of the AI industry.

VMware has also undergone a massive transformation since the acquisition from Broadcom, from a radical transformation designed to move from a fragmented, transactional software vendor to a streamlined, high-margin subscription engine. Thus it is following quite closely the Broadcom playbook focusing on maximizing profitability.

Valuation

Broadcom is clearly not cheap considering its valuation metrics with its P/E Non-GAAP of 43.85, which is effectively higher than Nvidia at 37.38 and Marvell (MRVL) at 34.54. This high valuation also translates on the free cash flow side, as Broadcom only has a FCF yield of around 1.8%. Though in this case, I believe that it is important to have a broader view of the company’s operation and inherent valuation to see if there is a question of relative good valuation. Here is my sum of the part analysis:

As you can see through this exercise, AVGO is rightly priced in this particular case, though through a scenario-based approach, Broadcom still has chances of a fairly strong upside, considering that the bear case is only standing at $280 per share. I believe that in this case, this is classic buy a great business at a fair price.

Outside of the $100B custom AI silicon roadmap, I’m enthusiastic about the dominance in million-XPU clusters by AVGO as the industry shifts from proprietary interconnects to open Ethernet standards. Tomahawk 6, the first 102.4 Tbps Ethernet switch chip that will enable two-tier network designs, allowing to reduce latency and power consumption in massive data centers. More so, around 70% of new AI infrastructure is now opting for Ethernet protocols over Nvidia’s InfiniBand on which Broadcom holds over 80% market share.

On the margin front, AVGO is able to post a gross margin nearing 77%, which is higher than its peers and nearly unmatched in the space of large-cap firms. The company’s ability to convert revenue into free cash flow is also highly impressive, with a rate of 41.6%, providing the floor needed for the $10B repurchase program plus the dividend that has grown by an astonishing rate of 15% CAGR over the past 5 years.

Risks

First, I’ll talk about the broad industry’s bottleneck being TSMC which is the most critical choke point for semiconductors, but also HBM and printed circuit boards. If there were anything to happen to Taiwan, given the current macro landscape, this would be highly detrimental to Broadcom and the industry.

Broadcom’s AI revenue is also heavily focused on hyperscalers, mainly Alphabet, Meta and OpenAI. If any of these partners shift to in-house designs or reduce their capex, the $100B AI revenue target guidance would certainly falter through. This is particularly something to watch out for considering the high debt level that followed with the VMware acquisition, which presents a broader risk compared to other tech peers.

Though AVGO’s ASICs offer cost advantages to Nvidia’s GPUs, the CUDA ecosystem remains one of the best entrenched moats for the largest company in the world. The CUDA infrastructure has been built over the years and transitioning to custom silicon requires a lot of software re-engineering.

Conclusion

As I said, the valuation is not as attractive as many of my top picks, but in essence, you are paying a fair price for a great company and a great management team. The growth is absolutely mind-blowing all across the board, thanks to the great acquisitions Broadcom has made, plus the $100B AI revenue would drastically increase the company’s bottom-line, compressing the heightened P/E. I rate Broadcom a Buy.

data.title: PNC Financial Services: In A 17% Drawdown, The Focus Shifts Back To Fundamentals In April
data.description:
PNC Financial Services (PNC) is 17% off its recent record high. That’s about on par with drawdowns within the regional banking space and broader Financials sector. Bank bulls hope that the focus shifts from the macro and geopolitical unease to fundamentals, and we’ll get that chance in just a couple of weeks.

Bank earnings begin rolling in during the middle of April. PNC reports on Tax Day, and I expect solid results, despite macroeconomic pressures possibly building. I had a buy rating on PNC back in September of 2024. Shares are up 17% since then, dividends included. Today, I’ll provide a refreshed valuation and an updated look at the technicals.

PNC: A 17% Drawdown Is A Buying Opportunity

Back in January, PNC reported a strong set of quarterly results. Q4 GAAP EPS of $4.88 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $4.21, while revenue of $6.1 billion, up 9% from the same period last year, was a material $120 mill beat. The company tallied net interest income of $3.7 billion (2% YoY growth), and included lower funding costs, loan growth, and fixed-rate asset repricing.

Shares jumped 3.8% in the session that followed—the best earnings reaction going back to July of 2024. Looking ahead to the April 15 Q1 report, the options market prices in a somewhat high 4.9% earnings-related stock price swing based on the at-the-money straddle expiring soonest after the report. Shares of the $82 billion market cap Financials sector firm feature an elevated implied volatility reading of 37%, while short interest is low at 1.44%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, PNC delivered impressive numbers to wrap up 2025, including record net interest income for the full-year and fee growth that topped analyst estimates. Total net income of $7 billion paired with a 21% YoY EPS rise, while the company successfully integrated FirstBank assets into its portfolio. That deal is forecast to add about $1 to per-share earnings in FY 2027, which only further bolsters the profitability outlook.

I also liked that PNC demonstrated solid operating leverage (a higher rise in the bottom line compared to the top line), a key sign of operating efficiency.

FY 2025 Lookback

Looking ahead, PNC put out a bullish 2026 outlook, seeing an 11% increase in total revenue to about $25.6 billion and 14% net interest income growth, while aiming for 400 basis points of positive operating leverage. The company also signaled shareholder-friendly moves for early 2026, including plans to repurchase up to $700 million in common stock during the first quarter.

We’ll see what updates we get within the Q1 report and on the earnings. I hope that the management team keeps costs in check, though, as they announced record capex plans for the current year. If they can effectively incorporate new AI tools, that could further drive efficiency.

FY 2026 Outlook

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to rise a solid 12% in FY 2026 with comparable gains in the out year. By FY 2028, the bank could reach $22 in per-share earnings, all while revenue growth eases into the low-mid single digits. The sellside remains quite upbeat on the Pittsburgh-based bank, as evidenced by a high 18 EPS upgrades in the past 90 days, compared to just a lone downgrade.

PNC: Revenue, Earnings, Revisions

On valuation, I have key updates to my Q3 2024 analysis. If we assume normalized EPS of $19 on a forward-12-month basis and apply the historical average 12.11x price-to-earnings multiple, shares should trade near $230.

I’d also call out that PNC sells for very close to its usual price-to-book ratio. So, it’s not on clearance, but shares are attractively priced, in my view. Keep in mind that PNC pays a solid 3.3% dividend yield, too.

PNC: Reasonable P/E, High Yield

Key risks for PNC include an uncertain macro backdrop and fears of rising recession risks. Strains on small-business lending conditions is a clear downside revenue and earnings risk, while the reality that the Fed may not cut interest rates again this year has rattled the market.

The bank anticipated about two quarter-point cuts back in January—that will likely get revised in the April report. As always, competitive pressures are intense in the regional banking industry, and tighter financial conditions could weigh on the sector.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show a confirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Wednesday, April 15 BMO. The company hosts its Annual General Meeting on the following Wednesday.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares 10-15% to the cheap side ahead of the important Q1 earnings report, PNC’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that the stock is right near its long-term 200-day moving average. That trend-line indicator remains modestly on the rise, suggesting that the bulls control the primary trend. The 50dma has rolled over, however, so it’s clearly not an easy path higher.

I’d also note that the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph is now in a somewhat bearish area, below 40. I’d like to see the stock hold an uptrend support line on improved RSI momentum.

Also take a look at where resistance appears—very close to last Friday’s close. The $205-$210 range had been resistance, then became support. Now, with PNC sitting at $202, it’s likely to be a spot of potential resistance once again. Next support is down near $175.

PNC: Shares Testing Key Support

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on PNC. The super-regional bank appears attractive on valuation, even in the face of so many macroeconomic concerns. I assert that the focus shifts back to fundamentals once we get into earnings season, and that should be a boon for the stock.

data.title: Fed Chair Powell's Speech In Focus, Nike To Report Earnings
data.description:
Get ahead of the market by subscribing to Seeking Alpha's Wall Street Week Ahead, a preview of key events scheduled for the coming week. The newsletter keeps you informed of the biggest stories set to make headlines, including upcoming IPOs, investor days, earnings reports, and conference presentations.

Wall Street’s major market averages traded lower on Friday as U.S. President Donald Trump extended the pause on attacks against Iran’s energy infrastructure into April. Chip and AI-related stocks were largely in the red as the U.S.-Israel-Iran war neared a month and U.S. President Donald Trump extended the pause on attacks against Iran’s energy infrastructure into April. On the conflict front, President Trump described talks with Tehran as “going very well.” However, an Iranian official dismissed a reported U.S. proposal to end the conflict as “one-sided and unfair.”

A slew of major economic data is scheduled for the coming week. It will begin with Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell’s speech, where investors will look out for cues on the broader economy. Consumer confidence data, JOLTS job openings and Chicago PMI are due on Tuesday. On Wednesday, major economic data, including ADP nonfarm employment change for March, retail sales data for February, and S&P Global manufacturing PMI numbers for March, will be released. Initial jobless claims numbers are scheduled to be released on Thursday, with nonfarm payrolls and unemployment rate for March due on Friday.

Nike (NKE) and Beyond Meat (BYND) are among the companies reporting their results next week.

_______________________________________________________________

Earnings spotlight: Tuesday: Nike (NKE), Beyond Meat (BYND). See the full earnings calendar.

Earnings spotlight: Wednesday: Conagra (CAG). See the full earnings calendar.

Volatility watch: AXT (AXTI) and Bloom Energy (BE) are set up for a volatile week of trading based on options volume. The most overbought stocks per their 14-day relative strength index include Transcontinental (TCLAF), Kore Group (KORE), and Assertio Holdings (ASRT). The most oversold stocks per their 14-day Relative Strength Index include Vital Farms (VITL), American Vanguard (AVD), and FuboTV (FUBO). Short interest is elevated on car rental stocks Hertz Global (HTZ) and Avis Budget (CAR).

IPO watch: HMH Holding (HMH) is expected to price its IPO and begin trading. The IPO lockup expires on certain blocks of shares of Neptune Insurance (NP), Knorex (KNRX), Commercial Bancgroup (CBK), and Energys Group (ENGS). The quiet period expires on MiniMed Group (MMED) to free up analysts to post ratings.

Dividend watch: Companies that have an ex-dividend date coming next week include Micron (MU), Deere (DE), Nucor (NUE), and Cisco (CSCO).

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Duolingo's AI Splurge Is A Reason To Buy, Not Sell (Upgrade)
data.description:
For anyone who wants exposure to a language learning platform, one interesting company to consider buying into is Duolingo (DUOL). In the past, I used the platform. Although I no longer do, I recognize the value that it provides for those seeking out language proficiency. In the past, management has achieved some rather attractive growth. And moving forward, there is the expectation this trend will continue. On top of this, the company has a remarkable balance sheet, with no debt and cash equivalent to 24.3% of its market capitalization. And yet, over the last year now, shares have fallen 71.4%. Some of this was likely because the stock was legitimately overvalued at one point. Even strong growth can only justify so much upside. But now, shares actually look rather appealing, even though management is forecasting weakness on the bottom line for this year. The way I view it, however, that bottom line weakness is because of significant investments that management is making in future growth. And when you put it that way, it seems to me as though this makes for a good soft ‘buy’ candidate. That compares to the 'hold' I assigned it way back in July of 2022.

Trying out Duolingo

The management team at Duolingo describes the company as a technology business dedicated to promoting education when it comes to languages. This might not seem like all that special a space to play in. However, the company has turned the business into a thriving enterprise, with over 900 employees at the end of 2025. The firm has a market capitalization today of $4.70 billion. That is certainly nothing to scoff at. It has gotten to the size by providing, through its app and website, the opportunity to learn foreign languages.

This is actually a pretty big space, with one source estimating that by 2027, consumers will spend $123 billion a year on both online and offline language learning. The fact of the matter is that learning a language is becoming increasingly important in a world that continues to become more globalized as time goes on. It gives you an important tool in your arsenal that can be employed for not only personal and entertainment reasons, but for professional reasons as well. That's why the company has grown from a small language learning app to an ecosystem that offers over 250 language courses to an estimated 133.1 million monthly active users.

For context, this number is greater than the number of K-12 students that are currently taking a foreign language class in school. And one interesting data point that management has offered up is that there are currently more people learning certain languages like Irish and Hawaiian than there are native speakers of those languages across the entire globe. Of course, the company has not stopped at language only. That was just the starting point. It now offers courses in math, music, and even chess. As a very strong club level player myself, as well as a collector of premium chess sets and over 130 chess clocks, I can definitely appreciate the foray into that space. And what really distinguishes the company from other players in these markets is that it operates by employing bite-sized and on-demand lessons that make integration into your daily life easy and fun. In fact, the company said that its learners complete almost 2 billion exercises each day. And now with AI on the scene, there is the opportunity for additional growth moving forward.

In the chart above, you can see financial results for the last three completed fiscal years. During this time, revenue nearly doubled from $531.1 million to $1.04 billion. This allowed a surge in profits and cash flows as well. Net income skyrocketed from $16.1 million to $414.1 million. However, this is a bit deceptive since the 2025 figure was boosted by significant tax benefits. More accurate would be other profitability metrics. Trading cash flow is a prime example. Over the last three years, it grew from $153.6 million to $387.8 million. If we strip out changes in working capital, we would get a rise from $118.7 million to $338.9 million. And finally, EBITDA for the business increased from $93.7 million to $305.9 million.

This overall expansion for the company was fueled by a rise in the number of users on the platform. In the last three years, the number of monthly active users shot up from 88.4 million to 133.1 million. This comes as the company continues to add language courses and new features, not to mention other subjects like I mentioned earlier. The number of daily active users nearly doubled from 26.9 million to 52.7 million. This is a fantastic metric on its own. The company promotes a freemium business model where many of the features on its platform are completely free. However, the way that it monetizes is through paid subscriptions that provide an ad free experience and additional features.

This has proven to be immensely valuable to users. Over the last three years, we have seen a near doubling in the number of paid subscribers from 6.6 million to 12.2 million. This has pushed subscription bookings up from $495.5 million to $996.3 million. This accounted for the overwhelming majority of the surge in total bookings from $622 million to $1.16 billion. Management expects this overall trend to continue into 2026. They currently think that bookings will range between $1.274 billion and $1.298 billion. At the midpoint, this would mean a year over year rise of 11%. And with that, revenue should expand from $1.04 billion last year to between $1.197 billion and $1.221 billion.

This is fantastic in and of itself. But I would posit that one of the reasons why the stock price has dropped recently stems from the fact that management expects a small contraction in overall profitability. For 2026, the company anticipates EBITDA of between $299 million and $305 million. The midpoint there of $302 million would represent a small dip from the $305.9 million reported for last year. In all likelihood, this would mean a drop in adjusted operating cash flow from $338.9 million to $334.6 million.

This might seem disconcerting to say the least. However, management made clear in their latest investor presentation that they are allocating additional capital toward accelerating user growth and engagement. As a result of this, operating costs are forecasted to rise faster than revenue across all of their categories. The one exception to this is general and administrative expenses that should, in theory, remain unchanged or drop a bit in relation to revenue thanks to the additional scaling that the company is exhibiting. This is a good decline in overall profits that we are seeing. In essence, management is investing more heavily into functionality and other operational opportunities with the goal of increasing financial success in the long run. A lot of this seems to be dedicated to AI powered features that the company is rolling out. Examples of features that management mentioned in their annual report that include AI would be Roleplay, Explain My Answer, and Video Call. These types of opportunities unfortunately require higher investments in computing infrastructure and related costs. However, if implemented appropriately, they should boost the user experience and result in greater stickiness on the platform. Management expects these investments to grow the firm’s daily active user count to 100 million by 2028.

Another great thing about the company is the fact that it has one of the best balance sheets I have ever seen. You see, the company has a market capitalization today of $4.70 billion. It has no debt on its books and enjoys $1.14 billion of cash and cash equivalents. That significantly lowers the risk profile of the company. And it means that management has plenty of fuel with which to grow. It's unclear, because of how strong cash flow already is, if the cash balance will shrink during 2026 because of increased investments. But honestly, even if it did, I wouldn't have a problem with it.

Even if profitability remains flat moving forward, it's not like the stock is particularly pricey. In the chart above, you can see how the stock is valued using results from 2025 and estimates for 2026. While the price to operating cash flow multiple is not quite as low as I would like it to be, the EV to EBITDA multiple is particularly tantalizing for such a high quality growth machine that generates significant cash flow. As an example, the EBITDA margin for 2026 is on track to be 23.5%. That is quite hefty in the grand scheme of things and justifies a bit of a premium compared to if this were some low margin, slow growth machine.

Earlier in this article, I mentioned some AI oriented features that management has invested in. Some investors might be concerned that the rise of AI could pose an existential threat to a company like Duolingo. But I view the picture is being a bit different than that. Management has already made investments in AI on their own. But keep in mind that, before AI was even a thing, foreign currency translation and learning technologies were already prevalent. Google Translate is an example of the former, while Rosetta Stone is an example of the latter. If helping others to understand foreign language in a static manner was the value proposition of Duolingo, the company likely never would have grown to become the giant that it is today.

What is actually valuable are the network effects created by the ecosystem that the company has built up during its lifetime. While even these can be susceptible to new technological threats, the fact that the management team is taking AI seriously and treating it as an opportunity in order to boost its user base and product stickiness actually means that this should be viewed as a positive for the company. The more that management invests in things like AI, the more defensible the business model will become to outside threats that also employ AI. Out on top of this the fact that the company's cash position gives it the ability to pick up other technologies, as opposed to letting them grow only to deal with them as competitors later, and I feel as though the company is in a very solid position here.

Takeaway

The way I see things right now, Duolingo is a solid prospect. It's not a classic value play. Rather, I view it as a GARP candidate that is continuing to grow nicely and that is high quality in nature. In the long run, I expect to continued growth from the business, and based on how the stock is priced and its robust balance sheet, I believe that rating it a soft ‘buy’ is logical here.

data.title: EPHE: State Of Emergency, All Of ASEAN Exposed To Middle East Oil Supply
data.description:
The iShares MSCI Philippines ETF (EPHE) is a pretty interesting ETF. PE ratios are pretty low here for an economy that is probably above average as far as emerging economy profiles go. There is quite a lot of indexation to a port operator in the ETF which does index the ETF quite a bit to the intensity of trade with trade partners, which comes down to the important economic ties with China that do quite a lot to determine the Philippines' economic health. Other than that exposure, we are looking at a considerable financial exposure too that is probably not enjoying the market pressures and economic threats from the Iran war, which matter for Southeast Asia and China, but at least the yield curve is steepening, which should be good for the corporate books and the consumer books driven by things like car loans. It's a dragging market with a decent development profile and therefore could be primed to grow, but it's quite linked to Chinese fortunes, like much of the rest of SE Asia, and it has traded sideways consistent with the challenged deleveraging of China's economy. We think that the economic consequences of the Iran war may slow down China's recovery, but deleveraging for almost half a decade now, it's also about time that it starts recovering—some green shoots were showing. We think that will then lift the Philippines as well, and starting from a low valuation base, it may be a good way to ride those coattails as an ASEAN play.

But with oil dependence on the Middle East themselves, Philippines is exposed, which matters a lot for the industrial and financial exposures and also for the peso, in which their flows are denominated. It has declared a state of national emergency as well, with oil not that shored up, and planes could be grounded and oil generally unavailable.

EPHE Breakdown

Bear Case

The macro situation is that the Iran war poses serious threats to quite a few Southeast Asian countries and China, who are all very dependent on oil imports, specifically from the Middle East and through the Strait of Hormuz. The Philippines is in a state of emergency currently with guaranteed oil supply only going for a little over a month, thereafter depending on sputtering flows from the Middle East, where much of the oil comes from. The macro concern is pretty obvious. Higher oil prices threaten corporate profits and risk demand destruction. China had started to see industrial profit recovery, and this will slow it. The Philippines depends on Chinese export and import markets and its economic integration with China, as the rest of the ASEAN economies, depends as much on their domestic businesses as it does on China's, which has a lot of regional gravity. Higher oil prices are also a reinflation risk. These are all difficult-to-mitigate issues for the sectoral exposures in EPHE:

Sectorally, there is significant financial exposure and therefore quite a lot of exposure to full-service banks whose fortunes depend on good macro conditions. The pains of poor conditions show up in higher provisioning as delinquencies and credit issues increase. For foreign investors, the businesses are denominated locally, and therefore the FX matters. The Philippine Peso is not doing well, and the Philippines stock benchmarks are taking a hit as markets see a flight to safety occur, with the USD gaining after a poor streak on the debasement trade. For financials, the primary mitigant of the economic threat is that, at least with growth in categories like car loans and also business loans, they will be able to benefit from a steepening of the yield curve, with a considerably higher short- to medium-term rate profile than before due to the paused rate hike. The reasoning of the off-cycle central bank meeting and the indication of paused cuts is explicitly on account of the higher inflation and reinflation risks from the war.

For the industrial exposure, International Container Terminal Services (ICTEF) is 21.4% of the portfolio, which is a considerable skew to that specific holding. Half of the industrial exposure is accounted for by this stock. The concern here is general economic activity and the intensity of regional activity in a moment where oil prices, a key input, are becoming expensive or even somewhat unavailable. Countries like Japan, China, Hong Kong, and South Korea are all dependent on oil imports. These countries account for 40%+ of exports and imports. The higher prices per barrel, denominated in USD, also mean to meet needs more domestic currencies need to be sold, which is a downward vector on most of the ASEAN FX. While the port's business is infrastructural and altogether quite attractive, it is at the very least a growth risk, reflecting another disappointment in growth that followed the effects of the Liberation Day tariffs that threatened quite an important economic relationship with the US.

Other significant exposures are real estate, whose values are also dependent on a decent economy and would benefit from rate cuts, not likely in the current environment. The reinflation risks preclude any rate help, and real estate in general is going to a pro-cyclical category.

Bull Case

A steepening yield curve is good for a business that pays for shorter-term deposits to lend at longer-term rates - it lifts net interest margins. With a growth skew in the consumer credit world, the steepening in shorter horizons transmits the opportunity also to shorter-term credit, even credit card loans. The revision in short-term rates for the Philippines is particularly steep. This can help at least compensate the banking exposures for the higher risks.

The rest of the bull case rests on geopolitical hopes. Perhaps enough ships can pass through Hormuz despite the dangers and perhaps even be permitted by Iran to alleviate some of the acute supply risks. The oil futures curve is anticipating a normalization this year as well. Perhaps the war ends.

The market is also quite cheap in absolute and relative terms. With a Chinese recovery overdue, it's just a matter of time until growth gives more oxygen to the best in the region.

Bottom Line

Prior to this, compressed valuations, specifically the 9.6x PE (Indonesia, represented by (EIDO), is around 12x), relative to historical and other ASEAN nation levels, all meant there could be a value angle in the recovery. ASEAN is generally exposed to China, so as a recovery eventually plays out, it would be best to go with the cheapest market available. But the Iran war, where oil futures still show a late 2026 normalization, threatens these economies and their currencies enough that the hazard is quite real. There isn't really any sign that the war is ending soon, and oil continues to climb. The other issue we should mention is that corruption scandals have been hitting the country recently and are a source of quite a lot of concern as it relates to critical infrastructure in a disaster prone country. This is an FDI threat and therefore an FX threat, and a concrete impairment to the Philippines economy, as the magnitude of the corruption could be quite significant. It's also, for the moment, a civil unrest risk. In terms of expense ratio, it has one in line with EIDO which reflects a relatively good service for a pretty exotic ETF. But in all, these are quite hard-hit economies; the currency is marginal and the selling pressures considerable, with meaningful supply shocks that could start slowing down the intensity of trade for the industrial exposures and weaken financial and real estate bets. This isn't risk free even if better compensated relatively in the region due to the valuation case.

data.title: The Greatest Risk To Your Portfolio Is You
data.description:
Co-authored with Beyond Saving

Investing is easier than ever. You can access your brokerage account, transfer some funds, type in a ticker, and buy X shares in ACME Corp.

Investing is harder than ever. You log into your phone, and the stock market is down! The news is saying that things are going to get a lot worse. High energy costs, war, a declining labor market, and private credit collapsing, and we haven't even gotten to the bad news yet. They call it "doom scrolling" for a reason. And you can find a ton of reasons not to invest right now.

Technology has made investing more accessible to the average person. It is easier than ever for a person to start investing, and it can be done with a lower amount of initial capital. Investment strategies like options used to be the purview of sophisticated Wall Street professionals. Today, it's a 20-something on Robinhood who just checked a box agreeing that they are sophisticated.

While technology has increased the ease and availability of investing, it has increased the risks. The greatest risk of them all? Take a look in the mirror.

Let's look at the biggest mistakes investors make.

1. Failing To Recognize That The Stock Market Is Volatile

Stock prices are volatile. It isn't uncommon for the stock market to move 1%-2% in a day. Over longer periods, it isn't unusual for the stock market to sell off by large amounts.

Consider someone who invested $1 million in 1993, an amount of money that back then was widely regarded as life-changing money. And 1993 was a great time to invest.

The investor saw their balance run up over $3.2 million! Party on, Wayne!

Then over the next three years, the investor saw their principal cut in half. Then another rally back up, only to be cut in half again.

In the abstract, it is easy to understand that equities can and do decline 50% sometimes. We can look at a chart today, and those dips look small and like no big deal:

Yet, the psychological reality is that it was a decade of zero returns and worse, for much of 2000-2010, stocks were down a lot. Investors, by and large, didn't just hold through it. They sold. That's why the S&P 500 index went down, because people were more eager to sell than they were to buy.

At the end of the day, whatever number is represented by 30% of your portfolio is probably "a lot" of money to you.

The S&P 500 has been on a historic run recently. Sure, there was that dip during COVID, and there was a mild bear market in 2022, but despite those speed bumps that proved relatively short-lived, the S&P 500 is closing in on one of its most profitable periods ever.

Over the past 15 years, the S&P 500 has had a CAGR of approximately 13%. That is well above its average, which, since 1985, the average 15-year return for the S&P 500 has been 9.45%, with a low of 3.64%:

The performance we've seen over the past 15 years has been stronger than usual. Unfortunately, the result seems to be that many investors today haven't known anything different, and they feel invincible.

What happens when they find out differently? The risk is that they panic and sell.

2. Putting Everything Into Equities

Buying common equities has become relatively easy. Buying bonds and other debt investments is much more difficult. The result is that many investors have portfolios that are primarily invested in equities. Often, we will discuss preferred equities, bonds, or funds that invest in debt, and we will get feedback along the lines of:

"Why would I invest in that? It has dramatically underperformed the S&P 500!"

This is especially true if you're looking back from today, where debt investments saw substantial sell-offs due to rising interest rates. The recent performance of bonds has been poor relative to equities.

In terms of total return, equities have more upside potential. They always have, and they always will have more upside potential. This is because equities take on greater risks.

Taking on more risk is wonderful if you are going through a period where equities are having higher-than-average returns. Yet as we discussed above, these returns are neither typical nor guaranteed to continue.

Having a portion of your portfolio dedicated to debt isn't going to produce the best results in most years. However, it will decrease your volatility and, more importantly, improve the cash flow of your portfolio, ensuring that you aren't forced to sell your equities at low prices.

We recommend holding a variety of investments beyond just equities, including traditional bonds, baby bonds, preferred equity, bond funds, and companies that invest in debt like mortgage REITs.

3. Not Having A Plan

Too many investors go into the market without a plan. They buy stocks but don't really have an underlying strategy. When they read something positive about a stock, they buy it. When they read something negative, they sell it. Often finding themselves buying high when stocks are popular and selling low when they are unpopular.

The result is that they often find themselves chasing what is popular today, jumping into crowded trades, and always being late. There are investment strategies that seek to profit from the ebbs and flows of popularity. If you are going to try to do that, you need to make sure that you are ahead of the crowd, not behind it.

The end result of not having a plan is that when met with market turmoil, you will sell.

The Solution

Regular readers of mine know what our solution is. We call it the "Income Method", and it involves investing in investments with a focus on cash flow. This strategy helps resolve the problems above. How?

  1. Our focus is on the cash flow that our portfolio is producing. As a result, the day-to-day volatility of prices doesn't play a major role in our decision-making. The income we withdraw from the portfolio is funded by interest and dividends, not relying on selling at high prices. In fact, we look forward to opportunities to reinvest a portion of our dividends at lower prices.
  2. As an income-centric strategy, we naturally are biased toward investment classes that produce interest or dividends. Fixed-income and debt-focused funds are holdings that produce cash flow.
  3. We have a plan that accounts for the types of investments we will buy, the yield we are targeting, and our goal of 8%-10% current yield. Our plan includes guardrails like allocation limits and diversification.

Is that the only possible plan? Of course not. Investing is an open slate that allows investors to make money using numerous different strategies. However, it is crucial to make sure your strategy considers the volatility of the market, diversifies beyond just common equities, and that you are pursuing a plan that you are capable of executing.

Conclusion

Volatility is picking up in the market, and we're seeing the market reacting to global events, which by definition are unpredictable. Will the market crash? Absolutely. It is an inevitable reality that is the equivalent of asking whether it will snow in Alaska. Will it happen in 2026? That is the harder question to answer.

It is important for us to recognize that equities are volatile and that wild price swings aren't something to be feared. They should be expected, and investors need to approach the market with a healthy mindset to deal with the mental stress of those swings.

Part of that is having assets that are outside common equities. We like to hold bonds, baby bonds, preferred equities, and debt funds.

Ultimately, it comes down to having a plan that is going to account for the realities of the market, while also accounting for your personal emotions. Many investors panic, they see red, and they sell. Realizing losses when they should be looking for opportunities to buy.

The greatest barrier to your investing success is looking at you in the mirror. If you can handle your emotions and make principled decisions in the face of adversity, you will discover investing success.
